notes.note_text:
Parent report:
- Mother reported that intelligibility is "hit or miss" at home; family understands most of what he says, but unfamiliar listeners often ask for repetition.
- She notices he often leaves off ending sounds and says "tee" for "key" and "tat" for "cat."
- No concerns with hearing, recent cold resolved.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned with mild hesitation but entered therapy room with hand-held support.
- Once in the room, he engaged well with preferred toys (cars, animal figures) and responded to visual schedules.
- Attention was fair; needed intermittent redirection from self-directed play back to speech tasks.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ in isolation and syllables
- Established /k/ in isolation with simultaneous production and visual cueing (open mouth, “tongue back” gesture). Client achieved consistent productions in isolation with maximal verbal and visual support.
- In simple CV syllables ("kuh, kee, koh"), accuracy was emerging; client often substituted /t/ but could correct with immediate models and jaw/tongue placement reminders.

2. Final consonants (p, t, m) in CVC words – imitation
- Targeted final /p, t, m/ in high-interest words (e.g., "cup, up, cat, hat, mom, ham") at the imitated word level.
- Frequent omission of final consonants; many productions reduced to CV forms ("cu" for "cup").
- With maximal verbal cues ("say the sound at the end") and tactile prompts on the table to “tap the last sound,” he produced some accurate final consonants, but consistency was low.

3. Initial /f/ – awareness and isolation
- Introduced /f/ using a mirror, focusing on “lip-bite and quiet air.”
- Client could imitate a sustained /f/ with clinician modeling but tended to voice it or stop to /p/ when adding a vowel.
- Needed continuous modeling and cueing; no independent /f/ productions observed in words yet.

4. Functional phrase attempts
- During play, client used several 2–3 word phrases (e.g., "my car," "more bubbles," "I want that") with reduced consonant accuracy.
- SLP modeled clearer productions and encouraged imitation of short functional phrases; client imitated some with variable accuracy but good willingness.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue establishing /k/ in isolation and CV syllables before moving to word level.
- Maintain focus on final /p, t, m/ in simple CVC words in imitation.
- Continue building awareness of /f/ with mirror work and airflow cues.
- Home practice: caregiver to model clear CVC words ("cup, cat, mop") in daily routines and encourage the child to “add the last sound,” praising any attempts rather than perfection.

Parent report:
- Mother shared that he is “trying hard” to copy words at home and will occasionally repeat after her when she slows down.
- She notices he sometimes says "tup" for "cup" now instead of just "cu," which she feels is new.
- No changes in health or sleep reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Smooth transition into session; greeted clinician spontaneously.
- Engaged well with turn-taking games; benefited from using a visual timer for task transitions.
- Mild silliness when tasks became more challenging, but redirected with structured choices.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – CV and word level in imitation
- Continued practice of /k/ in CV syllables and moved into simple words ("key, car, cow, cat").
- Accuracy in CV syllables improved with moderate cueing; fewer /t/ substitutions compared to last session.
- At word level, client produced initial /k/ correctly in several trials when given a model and “tongue back” cue, but consistency remained variable.

2. Final consonants (p, t, m) – CVC imitation
- Practiced final consonants using picture cards and a tapping cue for the last sound.
- Client produced final /p/ and /m/ more frequently today (e.g., "cup," "mom"), though often with reduced clarity.
- Final /t/ remained more challenging with frequent omission or substitution; required maximal verbal prompts and slowed rate.

3. Initial /f/ – isolation and CV syllables
- Reviewed /f/ in the mirror; client imitated sustained /f/ with less direct hand-over-hand support.
- In CV syllables ("foo, fee"), he produced several approximations with partial lip-bite and audible airflow, though voicing and stopping to /p/ still occurred.
- Needed frequent reminders to “bite your lip and blow.”

4. Functional phrases – imitation of 2–3 word combinations
- In structured play, client imitated short phrases ("my turn," "more bubbles," "big car") in ~2-word length.
- Consonant accuracy was inconsistent, but he showed more frequent attempts to copy the full phrase rather than only the final word.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue building consistency for initial /k/ at word level in imitation before advancing to phrases.
- Maintain work on final /p, t, m/ with visual and tapping cues; consider minimal pairs (e.g., "bee/beep") in upcoming sessions.
- Continue /f/ in isolation and CV syllables; do not advance to word level yet.
- Home practice: caregiver to use a mirror for "lip-bite" /f/ practice and to highlight final sounds in familiar words (e.g., “cuP,” “caT”) using gentle reminders.

Parent report:
- Mother reported “a little bit clearer” speech this week, particularly with words like "cup" and "mom."
- She notices he is starting to correct himself occasionally when she repeats his word back more clearly.
- No new medical or hearing concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client separated from caregiver with minimal prompting and quickly joined play.
- Highly engaged with picture card games and a fishing game; responded well to praise and sticker rewards.
- Attention to adult-directed tasks improved; required fewer prompts to stay seated during table work.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word level imitation and emerging phrases
- Targeted initial /k/ words ("key, car, cow, cat, cookie, coat") at the imitated word level.
- Accuracy in imitation was noticeably better than previous sessions; fewer /t/ substitutions and more consistent tongue-back placement with only verbal cues.
- Began introducing short carrier phrases ("my key," "go car") with imitation. Client was able to maintain /k/ in initial position in several phrase attempts when given a model.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC words
- Continued work on final consonants using picture naming in imitation.
- Final /p/ and /m/ showed increased consistency, with more words produced as full CVCs rather than CV.
- Final /t/ still the most difficult; however, he produced a few correct final /t/ sounds (e.g., "hat") when given slowed models and a “tap the last sound” cue.

3. Initial /f/ – CV and word-level imitation (emerging)
- Practiced /f/ in CV syllables and introduced a small set of familiar words ("fish, four, feet").
- Client produced several approximations with clearer lip-bite and airflow, requiring moderate cues but less physical guidance.
- In words, accuracy was inconsistent; some productions were closer to /f/ while others stopped to /p/ or were omitted.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases – imitation with speech clarity focus
- Used play routines to elicit short phrases ("more bubbles," "big ball," "my turn please") in imitation.
- Client was more willing to repeat full phrases; speech remained reduced in consonant accuracy but showed increased attempts at full phrase length.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Given improved accuracy, continue practicing initial /k/ at the imitated word level and expand carrier phrases.
- Maintain final /p, t, m/ practice; consider increasing contrast (e.g., minimal pairs) for final /t/.
- Continue shaping /f/ at CV and simple word level with mirror and airflow cues.
- Home practice: encourage short carrier phrases containing /k/ (e.g., "my car," "go car") and reinforce any attempts at final sounds with specific praise ("I heard your /p/ sound at the end!").

Parent report:
- Mother shared that he is saying "car" and "cookie" more clearly and that grandparents commented on understanding him a bit better.
- She reports he sometimes says "tar" first and then corrects to "car" when she models it.
- No changes in routine or health.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client entered room independently and initiated play with cars.
- Very cooperative with structured tasks; enjoyed turn-taking and was motivated by earning turns with a toy garage.
- Sustained attention for short table-top tasks without significant resistance.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word level and short phrases
- Targeted initial /k/ in words and 2–3 word phrases ("car," "cow," "cookie," "my car," "big cow").
- Client produced /k/ accurately in many imitated words with only light verbal cues; occasional /t/ substitutions were present but less frequent.
- In short carrier phrases, he was able to maintain /k/ in initial position in a growing number of attempts, though consistency dropped slightly compared to single words.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC imitation and emerging independence
- Continued CVC practice with picture cards and play-based tasks.
- Final /p/ and /m/ continued to improve; client sometimes produced these endings without a direct cue when highly familiar words were used (e.g., "up," "mom").
- Final /t/ remained variable; he produced correct final /t/ on several trials with maximal prompts but still omitted it frequently.

3. Initial /f/ – word-level imitation
- Practiced /f/ in words ("fish, feet, four, food") in imitation.
- Accuracy showed slight improvement; client more consistently used lip-bite positioning with visual cues.
- Still required frequent reminders for airflow and to avoid voicing; a few approximations were close enough to be understood as target words in context.

4. Functional phrases – 2–3 word combinations
- Used play routines (feeding animals, car wash) to elicit functional phrases ("wash car," "more food," "big fish").
- Client imitated a range of 2–3 word phrases, with emerging attempts to use similar phrases spontaneously in play.
- Consonant errors persisted, but he demonstrated willingness to try and responded well to corrective models.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Maintain focus on initial /k/ at word and short phrase level; consider probing for more independent productions in play.
- Continue final /p, t, m/ with emphasis on increasing consistency for final /t/ using slowed rate and tapping cues.
- Keep practicing initial /f/ in words with mirror and verbal cues; not yet ready for phrases.
- Home practice: encourage repeated practice of a few familiar /k/ words ("car," "cookie") in simple phrases and continue highlighting final sounds in daily routines (e.g., "up," "in," "out").

Parent report:
- Mother noted that speech clarity seems “about the same” as last week.
- She continues to hear some clearer "car" and "cup" productions but also many approximations.
- No concerns regarding behavior or sleep.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned smoothly; briefly checked back for mom, then engaged with clinician.
- Participated in both floor play and table tasks; needed occasional movement breaks but returned to tasks with prompts.
- Demonstrated good turn-taking with the clinician and responded to visual reinforcement.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word and phrase level imitation
- Repeated practice with initial /k/ in words and short phrases similar to last session.
- Performance was generally consistent with previous week; produced many /k/ sounds correctly in imitation with light to moderate verbal cues.
- Continued to show some /t/ substitutions, particularly when excited or speaking quickly.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC words
- Continued targeting final consonants in familiar CVC words.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained relatively stronger; client produced several accurate CVC forms in imitation.
- Final /t/ remained inconsistent, with some correct attempts under heavy cueing but frequent omissions.

3. Initial /f/ – word-level imitation
- Practiced /f/ words ("fish, feet, food") with mirror support.
- Accuracy and cueing needs similar to last week; client continued to require visual and verbal reminders for lip-bite and airflow.
- Some approximations were understandable in context but remained inconsistent.

4. Functional phrases – 2–3 words in imitation
- Used play scenarios to elicit short phrases ("my car," "more bubbles," "big ball").
- Client imitated a range of 2–3 word phrases with similar consonant accuracy to last session.
- No notable increase in independence, but he remained cooperative and willing to try.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue practicing initial /k/ at word and short phrase levels, aiming for greater consistency and beginning to reduce cues where possible.
- Maintain focus on final /p, t, m/, especially supporting final /t/ through slowed rate and tactile/visual cues.
- Ongoing /f/ word-level practice; consider incorporating simple minimal pairs (e.g., "fish/dish") once accuracy improves.
- Home practice: reinforce a small set of target words with initial /k/ and final consonants during daily routines, keeping practice brief and positive.

Parent report:
- Mother reported no major changes in speech this week; "some words are clear, some are still hard."
- She feels he is more confident and talks a lot, even when not fully understood.
- No new medical issues.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Quick transition and good rapport with clinician.
- Initially high energy with some silliness, which was redirected using visual rules and choice-making.
- Once settled, he participated well in structured play and tolerated short table tasks.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word and phrase level imitation
- Practiced initial /k/ in familiar words and short phrases.
- Accuracy in imitation remained relatively stable compared to prior sessions; he continued to need light verbal reminders for tongue-back placement.
- Occasional /t/ substitutions and omissions occurred, especially when attempting longer phrases.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC in imitation
- Continued focus on final consonants with picture cards and play.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained more consistent; produced several accurate CVC words when given a model.
- Final /t/ still required maximal cueing; only a few clearly correct final /t/ productions were observed.

3. Initial /f/ – word-level imitation
- Reviewed /f/ words with mirror and airflow cues.
- Performance similar to previous sessions; produced some clearer /f/ approximations but still frequently stopped to /p/ or omitted the sound.
- Needed consistent visual and verbal prompts for correct placement.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases – imitation
- Elicited short functional phrases ("more bubbles," "my turn," "big car") in imitation during play.
- Phrase length and participation were stable compared to last week; consonant accuracy remained reduced but he was willing to repeat.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue to consolidate skills for initial /k/ at word and phrase levels before advancing.
- Maintain emphasis on final consonant production, particularly final /t/, using multisensory cues.
- Ongoing work on initial /f/ with focus on consistent placement and airflow.
- Home practice: caregivers to continue modeling and gently prompting target words, keeping expectations flexible and focusing on positive reinforcement.

Parent report:
- Mother reported that his talking has been “about the same” this week.
- She notices he is very talkative but still hard to understand for people who don’t know him.
- No changes in home routine or health.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Smooth transition; greeted clinician and chose a preferred toy.
- Cooperative overall; a bit distractible when less preferred tasks were introduced, but responded to visual schedule and first/then language.
- Enjoyed interactive games and responded well to specific praise.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word and short phrase imitation
- Continued work on initial /k/ in single words and simple phrases.
- Accuracy in imitation was similar to recent sessions; he produced /k/ correctly in many trials with light cues, though substitutions persisted intermittently.
- Maintained /k/ in initial position in some short phrases when closely supported.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC imitation
- Targeted final consonants in familiar CVC words.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained relatively strong in imitation; final /t/ continued to be the weakest and often omitted.
- No significant change in cueing needs or consistency compared to previous weeks.

3. Initial /f/ – word-level imitation
- Practiced initial /f/ words using mirror and gesture cues.
- Performance remained generally consistent; some correct or close approximations, but many productions still required heavy support.

4. Functional phrases – 2–3 words in imitation
- Used play-based routines to elicit short phrases focused on target sounds (e.g., "go car," "big fish," "cup up").
- Client imitated a variety of 2–3 word phrases with similar accuracy to prior sessions; continued to benefit from clear models and repetition.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue consolidating initial /k/ at current levels while monitoring for opportunities to reduce cues.
- Maintain practice on final /p, t, m/, with extra support for final /t/.
- Ongoing work on initial /f/ in words with intensive visual and verbal cueing.
- Home practice: encourage brief daily practice with a few target words (e.g., "car," "cup," "fish"), using play and routines to keep practice motivating.

Parent report:
- Mother stated that his speech “seems similar” to the last few weeks.
- She feels he is more willing to repeat when she asks, but clarity for strangers is still limited.
- No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned independently and quickly joined the clinician in the therapy room.
- Participated in both play and structured tasks; needed occasional prompts to stay on topic.
- Responded positively to token rewards and verbal praise.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word and phrase level
- Practiced initial /k/ in a familiar set of words and short phrases.
- Accuracy remained fairly stable; he continued to produce correct /k/ in many imitated words with light cueing.
- Phrase-level productions showed similar patterns to recent sessions, with some correct /k/ carryover and some reversion to /t/.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC in imitation
- Continued CVC practice using picture naming and play-based tasks.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained more consistent; final /t/ continued to require high levels of support.
- Overall performance similar to previous weeks with no major shift in independence.

3. Initial /f/ – word-level imitation
- Reviewed initial /f/ words with mirror and verbal cues.
- Client produced several approximations, but still needed frequent reminders for lip-bite and airflow.
- Accuracy and cueing demands were comparable to prior sessions.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases – imitation
- Elicited short phrases in routines (e.g., "more bubbles," "big car," "wash feet").
- Phrase length and willingness to imitate were stable; consonant accuracy remained reduced but consistent with previous notes.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue reinforcing initial /k/ at word and phrase level while watching for opportunities to fade cues.
- Maintain focus on final consonants, especially final /t/, with multisensory cues.
- Ongoing /f/ work at word level; not yet ready to move to phrases.
- Home practice: caregivers to continue modeling target words and short phrases, praising any attempts at final sounds and /k/ or /f/ productions.

Parent report:
- Mother reported that speech is “pretty steady” and that he is very chatty at home.
- She notes he still leaves off the ends of many words, but she is starting to hear clearer "mom" and "cup" more often.
- No new health or behavior concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Client transitioned without difficulty and chose to start with a car track activity.
- Cooperative and engaged throughout most of the session; benefited from occasional movement breaks between table tasks.
- Followed simple directions and responded well to visual supports.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word and phrase level
- Targeted initial /k/ in previously practiced words and short phrases.
- Accuracy and cueing needs were similar to recent weeks; he produced many correct /k/ sounds in imitation but continued to show inconsistent carryover into phrases.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC imitation
- Continued practice of final consonants using picture cards and play routines.
- Final /p/ and /m/ remained more consistent in imitation; some spontaneous final /p/ productions noted in play (e.g., "up").
- Final /t/ remained inconsistent and often omitted even with cues.

3. Initial /f/ – word-level imitation
- Practiced initial /f/ words ("fish," "feet," "four") using mirror, visual, and verbal cues.
- Overall performance remained stable; he was able to approximate /f/ in some trials but continued to require close support for accurate productions.

4. Functional phrases – 2–3 word combinations in imitation
- Used familiar play routines to elicit short phrases ("my car," "more fish," "big cup").
- Client imitated phrases with similar length and accuracy as in prior sessions; remained motivated and willing to participate.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue current level of practice for initial /k/ and final consonants, reinforcing consistent productions and beginning to highlight self-corrections.
- Maintain /f/ word-level work with intensive cueing.
- Home practice: encourage frequent, brief practice of target words in natural routines (e.g., "cup up," "big car"), focusing on positive feedback rather than correction.

Parent report:
- Mother reported that speech continues to be similar overall, though she feels he is "trying to fix" his words more when she repeats them.
- She has noticed clearer "car" and "cup" in some contexts.
- No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned easily and followed the visual schedule.
- Good engagement with both floor and table activities; brief periods of silliness when tasks were challenging but redirected with choices and humor.
- Demonstrated good rapport with clinician and responded to descriptive praise.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word and short phrase level
- Practiced a familiar set of initial /k/ words and 2–3 word phrases.
- Performance was consistent with prior sessions; produced many accurate /k/ sounds in imitation with light cues.
- Continued to show some difficulty maintaining /k/ in longer phrases or when speaking quickly.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC imitation
- Continued work on final /p, t, m/ in simple words.
- Final /p/ and /m/ productions remained more reliable; final /t/ persisted as the most difficult, with omissions common.
- Cueing and overall accuracy remained similar to last week.

3. Initial /f/ – word-level imitation
- Reviewed /f/ words with mirror and airflow cues.
- Client produced a few clearer /f/ productions but still required ongoing verbal and visual support; overall accuracy similar to previous sessions.

4. Functional phrases – 2–3 words in imitation
- Elicited short phrases during play ("my car," "more bubbles," "big fish").
- Phrase production and participation were stable; consonant accuracy remained reduced but in line with recent performance.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue consolidating skills at current levels for initial /k/, final consonants, and /f/.
- Monitor for spontaneous use of target sounds in play to determine readiness for advancing tasks.
- Home practice: maintain short, play-based practice on target words and phrases, with emphasis on encouragement and noticing any self-corrections.

Parent report:
- Mother reported that overall speech seems similar, but she has started hearing him say "cup" and "up" with clearer final /p/ more often without being asked.
- She feels he is more aware when words sound “funny” and will sometimes try again.
- No medical or behavioral concerns reported.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Smooth transition and eager participation; brought a toy car from home to show the clinician.
- Attentive and cooperative; tolerated longer stretches of structured tasks before needing breaks.
- Demonstrated good turn-taking and followed simple instructions.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – word and phrase level, emerging independence
- Practiced initial /k/ words and short phrases.
- Client produced /k/ accurately in most imitated words with minimal verbal cues; fewer /t/ substitutions were noted.
- In play, he began to use some /k/ words (e.g., "car," "cookie") more independently, though consistency still varied.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC in imitation and emerging spontaneous use
- Continued CVC practice with picture cards and play scenarios.
- Final /p/ and /m/ showed more consistent accuracy, and some spontaneous final /p/ productions (e.g., "up," "cup") were heard without direct prompting.
- Final /t/ remained more challenging, but he produced several correct final /t/ sounds with slowed models and tapping cues.

3. Initial /f/ – word-level imitation
- Practiced /f/ words ("fish, feet, food") using mirror and placement cues.
- Slight increase in consistency of lip-bite and airflow; still required support but demonstrated more frequent accurate or close approximations.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases – imitation with emerging clarity
- Used play-based routines to elicit phrases incorporating target sounds (e.g., "my car," "cup up," "big fish").
- Client imitated phrases with improved consistency in maintaining target sounds within the phrase in some trials.
- Continued to respond well to corrective feedback and modeling.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Given emerging independence, continue practicing initial /k/ at word and phrase level, gradually probing for more spontaneous use in play.
- Maintain focus on final /p, t, m/, supporting increased spontaneous final /p/ and /m/, and continue intensive support for final /t/.
- Continue /f/ word-level practice; consider probing simple two-word phrases with /f/ as accuracy allows.
- Home practice: encourage use of target words in daily routines (e.g., "cup," "up," "car"), praising any self-corrections or attempts at final sounds.

Parent report:
- Mother shared that family members have commented that some of his words are easier to understand, especially "car," "cup," and "mom."
- She notices he is starting to correct himself more often when she repeats a word back clearly.
- No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Transitioned independently and appeared excited to participate.
- Highly engaged with both structured and play-based tasks; needed minimal redirection.
- Demonstrated good persistence even with more challenging activities.

Goals / Observations:
1. Initial /k/ – phrases and early spontaneous use
- Targeted initial /k/ in words and 2–3 word phrases ("my car," "go car," "big cookie").
- Client produced /k/ accurately in most imitated words with minimal cues and maintained /k/ in many short phrases.
- During free play, he spontaneously used several clear /k/ words (e.g., "car," "cow") without prompting, indicating generalization.

2. Final consonants /p, t, m/ – CVC in imitation and spontaneous contexts
- Continued CVC practice and monitored for spontaneous use.
- Final /p/ and /m/ were produced more consistently in both imitated and some spontaneous words (e.g., "up," "cup," "mom").
- Final /t/ remained less consistent but showed improvement; he produced more accurate final /t/ in imitation (e.g., "hat," "cut") with moderate cues.

3. Initial /f/ – word level and trial two-word phrases
- Practiced initial /f/ words and introduced simple two-word phrases (e.g., "fish up," "fun car") in imitation.
- Client demonstrated more reliable lip-bite and airflow for /f/ in words with moderate cues and was able to carry /f/ into a few short phrases when strongly supported.
- Accuracy is improving but not yet consistent without cues.

4. Functional phrases – 2–3 word combinations with target sounds
- Used structured play to elicit functional phrases containing target sounds ("my car," "cup up," "big fish," "more food").
- Client imitated a range of 2–3 word phrases and showed improved ability to maintain target sounds within the phrase in supported contexts.
- Some spontaneous use of short phrases with clearer target sounds was observed during play.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue to build on gains with initial /k/, increasing expectations for spontaneous use in conversation and expanding to more varied vocabulary.
- Maintain work on final /p, t, m/, with particular focus on stabilizing final /t/ in familiar CVC words.
- Further develop initial /f/ at word level and short phrases, gradually reducing cueing as accuracy improves.
- Home practice: caregivers to emphasize everyday opportunities to use target words and short phrases (e.g., "my car," "cup up," "big fish"), providing specific praise for clear sounds and gentle reminders as needed.

Session 1

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Caregiver reported that child is "hard to understand" for unfamiliar listeners and often leaves off sounds at the ends of words.
- No previous speech therapy. Caregiver noted child will sometimes repeat words if asked but gets "silly" when corrected.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child transitioned into the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from caregiver with reassurance.
- Once settled, engaged well in play-based activities and responded to visual games and toys.
- Attention to adult-directed tasks was brief (2–3 minutes) and required frequent redirection from self-directed play.

Goals / Treatment Focus:
1. Establish awareness of final consonants (CVC words)
   - Used picture cards and simple toys to elicit CVC words (e.g., "cup," "bus," "bed").
   - Child frequently omitted final consonants in spontaneous attempts (e.g., "bu" for "bus").
   - With direct imitation and visual cueing (touching mouth, pointing to final sound icon), child produced the final consonant in some trials, but productions were inconsistent and often reduced to a vowel-consonant blend.

2. Initial /k/ at syllable and simple word level (imitation)
   - Practiced /k/ in isolation using "back sound" cues (e.g., "tongue goes in the back").
   - Child was able to approximate /k/ in isolation in about half of attempts, with frequent substitution of /t/.
   - At syllable level ("koo," "kah") and in simple words ("car," "key"), productions were mostly /t/; occasional correct productions with simultaneous model and verbal cueing.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
   - Introduced /s/ as a "long, quiet snake sound" with visual model and mirror.
   - Child could prolong a "hissy" sound in isolation with heavy support, but there was frequent stopping or distortion.
   - Attempts in CV syllables ("see," "soo") were mostly reduced to /t/ or /d/; occasional closer approximations when watching clinician's mouth.

4. Baseline observation of overall speech sound patterns
   - In play-based conversation, child showed frequent final consonant deletion, fronting of /k/ and /g/ to /t/ and /d/, and variable vowel clarity.
   - Syllable shapes heard: V, CV, CVCV; CVC was emerging but inconsistent.

Plan / Recommendations:
- Continue to build awareness of final consonants and back sounds (/k/) at the imitation level.
- Use simple CVC words in play at home; caregiver to model and gently emphasize final sound (e.g., "cuP," "buS") without pressuring child to repeat.
- Next session: re-check /k/ and final consonants; consider introducing visual markers (tokens/picture cues) for beginning vs. ending sounds.

Session 2

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Caregiver reported no major changes since the first visit.
- Noted that child sometimes tries to copy "back sound" /k/ when prompted but "it usually sounds like a t."

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child separated from caregiver with minimal prompting.
- More comfortable in the room today; explored toys and initiated some play themes.
- Still needed reminders to stay with the task but tolerated short, structured activities between play turns.

Goals / Treatment Focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation)
   - Target words: "bus, bed, cup, map, moon" and similar.
   - Child continued to omit many final consonants in spontaneous attempts.
   - With direct imitation and visual cueing (pointing to picture of "end" of word), child produced some final /p/ and /m/ with clearer articulation, though accuracy remained inconsistent.
   - Occasional self-correction when clinician emphasized the final sound.

2. Initial /k/ at syllable and word level
   - Revisited /k/ in isolation using mirror and tongue placement cues.
   - Child demonstrated similar performance to last session: frequent fronting to /t/ and needed maximal verbal and visual cues for back placement.
   - In words ("car," "key," "cow"), correct /k/ was rare and mostly occurred with simultaneous production and slowed rate.

Parent/caregiver report:

Behaviour/engagement:

Attention was variable; needed intermittent redirection back to speech activities but responded to visual supports and play-based routines.

Goals / session focus:
1. Establish production of /k/ and /g/ in isolation
- Targeted back sounds /k/ and /g/ in isolation using mirrors, visuals, and play with cars and animals. Child frequently substituted /t/ and /d/ for /k/ and /g/ (e.g., saying "t" for /k/). With simultaneous productions and maximal verbal/visual cueing ("tongue back, coughing sound"), child produced approximations of /k/ in ~3–4 attempts per 10 trials. /g/ was more difficult; mostly produced /d/ despite repeated modeling. Able to briefly feel the "back of the tongue" with clinician hand-over-hand to touch throat area, but carryover to sound production was inconsistent.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Introduced "long, quiet snake sound" /s/ in isolation. Child could imitate a prolonged /s/ in 4/10 attempts with strong visual cueing and clinician modeling. Productions were often too short or voiced (more like /z/). Trialed CV syllables ("see," "soo") in a few attempts; most responses were approximations with variable accuracy. Required direct imitation and maximal cues; no independent productions observed.

3. Final consonant awareness (CVC words)
- In play with picture cards (e.g., "bus," "cup," "cat"), child frequently omitted final consonants or reduced them to a weak vowel ("bu," "cu"). Clinician modeled clear final sounds and used tapping and segmenting. Child was able to imitate clearer final consonants in 2–3 opportunities per word with heavy support. No spontaneous final consonant productions in connected play.

4. Functional communication / phrase length observation
- During free play, child primarily used single words and short memorized phrases ("my turn," "I want"). Intelligibility in connected speech was reduced due to sound errors and omissions. Child attempted to repair breakdowns when not understood by repeating or pointing.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build consistency for /k/ and /g/ in isolation before moving to syllables. Maintain focus on establishing a stable /s/ in isolation and simple CV syllables. Continue modeling final consonants in simple CVC words.
- Home program: caregiver to practice "coughing sound" /k/ in front of the mirror using fun car/animal pictures, aiming for a few short, positive practice bursts per day. Encourage slow, clear models at home without pressuring the child.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Initial /f/ in CV and CVC words (imitation, emerging independence)
- Practiced /f/ in CV syllables and a small set of high-interest CVC words ("feet, fish, fun"). Child produced /f/ in CV syllables with noticeably improved consistency following a model, needing fewer verbal cues for placement.
- In CVC words, he was able to maintain initial /f/ in several attempts, though final consonant deletion was still present (e.g., "fi" for "fish"). Demonstrated emerging self-correction when clinician gently reminded him of "bunny teeth".

2. Initial /s/ in CV syllables and simple CVC words (imitation)
- Used snake puppet and visual cues. Child produced /s/ in CV syllables ("see, soo, suh") with slightly increased accuracy. He showed more attempts to sustain the /s/ without adding a /t/ or /d/ onset.
- Introduced a few simple CVC words with initial /s/ ("sun, soup"). Accuracy was variable; he often substituted /d/ or reduced the word. With slow modeling and gesture, he achieved some clearer productions.

3. Final consonant marking in CVC words (imitation with segmentation)
- Continued work on CVC words with focus on final /p/ and /m/ ("map, mop, nap, mom"). Used segmenting strategy ("mmm-aaa-p, map"). Child showed slightly more success adding the final consonant immediately after the clinician's segmented model.
- Spontaneous productions in play still often lacked final consonants, but within structured drills he was more willing to attempt full CVC shapes.

4. Functional phrases in play (imitation)
- Modeled short functional phrases ("my turn," "more please," "I want ___"). Child imitated 2–3 word phrases in the majority of opportunities, though speech clarity varied. Clinician provided recasts and gentle reminders for target sounds within these phrases when appropriate.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue with /f/ in initial CV and simple CVC words; consider increasing the number of CVC words if consistency remains.
- Maintain /s/ practice at CV level and a small selection of CVC words.
- Home: parent encouraged to focus on a few "/f/ words" (e.g., "feet, food, fish") during routines and to pause briefly to allow child to try the word after hearing a clear model.

Parent report:
- Mother reported that he is starting to copy her when she exaggerates sounds, especially /s/ in "snake" and /f/ in "fish." She notices small improvements but still feels others have difficulty understanding him in longer sentences.

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child arrived in good spirits and quickly joined the clinician at the table. He was chatty and eager to share about his day. Required some reminders to slow down and listen to directions, but overall engagement was strong.

Goals/therapy activities:
1. Initial /f/ in CVC words and short phrases (imitation, early carryover)
- Practiced initial /f/ in a set of CVC words ("fish, feet, fun, farm"). Child produced /f/ more reliably after a model; substitutions to /p/ were less frequent than in previous sessions.
- Began embedding /f/ words into short carrier phrases ("my ___," "I see ___") with visual support. Child was able to maintain the /f/ in some phrase-level attempts, though final consonants were still often omitted.

2. Initial /s/ in CVC words (imitation) and awareness of blends
- Targeted initial /s/ in words like "sun, soap, sock, soup" using picture cards. Child showed slightly increased accuracy compared to last week, particularly when given time to watch the clinician.
- Briefly introduced /s/ blends in a listening task (e.g., "star" vs. "tar"). Child could sometimes point to the correct picture but was not yet ready for consistent production of blends.

3. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation, visual cues)
- Continued structured practice with final /p, m, t/ in familiar CVC words. Used tapping strategy to mark each sound in the word.
- Child produced some clearer final consonants with direct modeling and tapping, especially for /p/ in "cup" and "up." However, carryover to spontaneous speech remained limited.

4. Functional 2–3 word phrases (imitation and emerging spontaneous use)
- Modeled functional phrases with picture support ("more bubbles," "my turn," "go fast"). Child imitated phrases in most opportunities and occasionally produced similar phrases spontaneously during play.
- Speech intelligibility in phrases was variable but showed slightly more consistent production of initial target sounds when cued.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue at current level for /f/ and /s/ in CVC words, with gradual increase in phrase-level practice as tolerated.
- Maintain focused practice on final consonants using tapping and slow models.
- Home: parent encouraged to practice short phrases containing /f/ and /s/ (e.g., "fun fish," "sun soap") in a playful, low-pressure manner.

Attention was variable; needed occasional redirection from self-directed play back to sound practice but responded to visual supports and short activities.

Goals / session focus:
1) Initial /f/ at isolation and syllable level
- Introduced /f/ using “quiet lip-bite, long air” cue with mirror. In isolation, child produced /f/ with moderate verbal and visual cues in approximately half of attempts; tended to substitute /p/ when cueing faded.
- At CV syllable level (e.g., “fuh,” “fee,” “foh”), accuracy decreased; often reverted to /p/ or omitted the consonant. Able to imitate a few accurate CVs when model and gesture were closely paired.

2) Initial /s/ in isolation and simple CV syllables
- Practiced “snake sound” /s/ in isolation with visual cue (finger in front of mouth). Child could sustain /s/ briefly in some trials but frequently produced /t/ or stopped the airflow.
- At CV level, productions were mostly /tV/ (e.g., “tee” for “see”). Needed maximal modeling and repetition. Occasional correct /s/ in isolation when attention was high.

3) Simple CVC words with final /p, m, t/ for overall consonant production
- During play, probed final consonants (e.g., “cup,” “map,” “hat”). Child omitted many final sounds, especially in spontaneous play. With direct imitation and tapping cue on table, produced some clearer final /p, m/ in single words.
- Accuracy inconsistent; still at early stage of learning to mark final sounds.

4) Early word combinations (2–3 word phrases) for functional communication
- In play, child used a mix of single words and short phrases (“my turn,” “more car,” “no mine”). Many productions were reduced or unclear, but intent was generally understood with context.
- Modeled clearer 2–3 word phrases and encouraged imitation. Child imitated several short phrases with support.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue targeting initial /f/ and /s/ at isolation and simple syllable level next session, focusing on establishing consistent airflow.
- Encourage caregiver to model slow, clear /f/ and /s/ words during daily routines (e.g., “fish,” “sun,” “soap”) without pressuring child to repeat.
- Plan to further probe final consonant production and monitor phrase length and intelligibility in play.

Goals / session focus:
1. Baseline for overall speech sound production
- Obtained an informal speech sample during play and picture naming. Noted frequent final consonant deletions (e.g., "ca" for "cat"), reduced consonant blends, and substitutions for /k/ and /g/ (fronting to /t, d/). Intelligibility reduced in connected speech, especially for unfamiliar topics.

2. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, m, t/)
- Introduced final consonant targets at the imitated word level using picture cards (e.g., "map, mop, mat, cup, ham"). Child often omitted final sounds even with models. With maximal verbal cues ("stop your sound," "close your lips"), could produce some clearer final consonants in isolation but had difficulty carrying over into words.

3. Initial /k/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Practiced /k/ in isolation using tongue-back placement cues and a mirror. Child produced a back sound inconsistently; often substituted /t/. With hand-over-hand placement cues on the throat and visual modeling, child approximated /k/ in isolation a few times. Limited success in CV syllables ("koo, kah"); required direct imitation and frequent reminders.

4. Early word shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Targeted simple syllable shapes within play (e.g., "moo, baa, baby, puppy"). Child produced many CV and CVCV forms spontaneously but often reduced accuracy on specific consonants. Focus was on encouraging any clear consonant-vowel pattern rather than specific phonemes.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build rapport and maintain focus on final consonants and initial /k/ at the imitated word and syllable level.
- Caregiver encouraged to model slow, clear CVC words in daily routines (e.g., "cup, top, bed") without pressuring child to repeat every time.
- Next session: recheck tolerance for mirror work and continue establishing consistent /k/ in isolation, as well as final /p/ and /t/ in high-interest words.

Phrases like "four fish" were attempted; accuracy was emerging but still inconsistent.

Therapy goals and observations:
Goal 1: Establish /k/ in isolation and simple syllables (back sound vs. front sound)
- Targeted /k/ in isolation using mirror, tongue depressor for awareness only (no placement), and visual of "back sound" vs. "front sound."
- Child often substituted /t/ for /k/ in spontaneous play. With direct imitation and models, child achieved a few accurate /k/ productions but required maximal verbal and visual cueing (e.g., "tongue up and back").
- Able to produce /k/ in isolation in approximately 3–4 clear attempts during the session; difficulty carrying over to syllables (e.g., "koo," "kah").

Goal 2: Initial /f/ at syllable and early word level (imitation)
- Introduced /f/ using "quiet lip air" cue and mirror work. Child tended to stop /f/ to /p/ in spontaneous attempts.
- In imitation of simple syllables ("foo, fee, fa"), child produced a few accurate productions but frequently reverted to /p/ or omitted the airflow.
- At word level with strong models ("fish, fun, four"), accuracy was low and required maximal verbal and gestural cueing.

Goal 3: CV/CVC syllable shapes for clarity in play
- Observed inconsistent marking of final consonants in CVC words during play (e.g., "ca_" for "cat," "do_" for "dog").
- Practiced a small set of high-interest CVC words ("dog, cat, hat, pop") in imitation. Child could approximate final consonants when given slowed rate and segmented models but did not yet produce them consistently.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to focus on establishing /k/ and /f/ at the isolation and simple syllable levels next session; maintain strong visual and verbal cueing.
- Encourage parent to model slow, clear productions of key words at home (e.g., "cat," "dog," "cup") without pressuring the child to repeat.
- Plan to gather additional baseline data on final consonant production and consider adding specific final consonant goals once more stable.

Therapy goals and observations:
1) Establish production of early consonants in isolation (p, b, m, t, d, k, g)
- Probed in play and structured imitation. /p, b, m, t, d/ generally present in simple CV syllables (e.g., "bee," "moo"), but final consonants were frequently omitted in CVC words (e.g., "boo" for "boat").
- /k, g/ were often fronted to /t, d/ in both imitation and spontaneous attempts (e.g., "tar" for "car").
- With direct modeling and gesture cues (tapping throat vs. lips), he could occasionally imitate /k/ in isolation but had difficulty carrying it into syllables.

2) Final consonant production at the imitated word level (CVC words)
- Targeted final /p, m, t/ using picture cards and simple games (e.g., "pop," "map," "cat").
- Frequently omitted final sounds or added a neutral vowel ("puh"). Needed maximal verbal cueing ("keep your sound on") and visual cues (pointing to lips) to mark the final consonant.
- Achieved a small number of clearly marked final consonants with simultaneous production (saying it together with SLP); independent productions were inconsistent.

3) Initial /f/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Introduced /f/ using visual cue of "bite your lip and blow." In isolation, he could approximate /f/ with heavy cueing but often substituted /p/ or produced a prolonged /h/ sound.
- In CV syllables ("foo," "fee"), productions were mostly /p/ or omitted the consonant. Required maximal modeling and repetition.

4) Initial /s/ in isolation
- Attempted /s/ in isolation with mirror and snake visuals. Productions were often /d/ or a lateralized sound; sustained airflow was difficult.
- Tolerated placement cues (smile, teeth together, long air) but needed hand-over-hand support for airflow.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to build consistent production of final consonants in CVC words and explore stimulability for /k, g, f, s/ at the isolation and syllable levels.
- Provide parent with simple home practice ideas for final sounds (e.g., "pop," "map," "hat") using slow, exaggerated models.
- Next session: further assess stimulability for /k/ and /f/, and begin a small set of core CVC words for practice.

- Client appeared comfortable and confident in the therapy setting. He engaged well in a mix of structured activities and free play, and he responded positively to feedback. He enjoyed listening to recordings of his own speech and identifying his "best sounds."

Goals / session targets:
1. Final consonant production in phrases and simple sentences
- Used a picture book and sequencing cards to elicit short sentences. Client consistently marked final consonants in many CVC words and maintained them in short sentences when given minimal prompts to slow down and "finish the word." Occasional omissions persisted in longer utterances, but overall performance was consistent with recent sessions, indicating stable gains.

2. Initial /k/ in connected speech and simple narratives
- Targeted /k/ within short narratives about cars and animals. Client produced many accurate initial /k/ words spontaneously and demonstrated good accuracy when repeating modeled sentences. Fronting to /t/ was infrequent and mainly occurred when he was excited or speaking quickly. Performance was similar to prior sessions and reflects maintained improvement compared to the start of therapy.

3. Initial /s/ at word level and short phrases
- Practiced initial /s/ in a set of familiar words ("sun, soap, soup, sock, seat") and short phrases ("see sun," "my sock"). Client continued to show variable accuracy, with some clear /s/ productions and some /sh/ or other substitutions. He responded to visual and verbal cues and could sometimes self-identify a "good snake sound," but consistency remains an ongoing target.

4. Overall intelligibility and functional communication
- In play and structured conversation, client used 3–5 word sentences frequently. Intelligibility to the clinician and, per caregiver report, to familiar listeners has improved since the beginning of the block. Remaining difficulties are most noticeable with /s/ and with reduced accuracy in longer, faster speech.

Plan / recommendations:
- Recommend continued speech-language therapy focusing on:
  - Maintaining gains in final consonant production and /k/ in increasingly spontaneous contexts.
  - Ongoing work on initial /s/ at the word and phrase levels to increase consistency.
- Home practice: encourage daily short "speech games" using target words and phrases, keep models slow and clear, and provide specific praise for good starting and ending sounds.
- Suggest sharing current progress and strategies with school staff to support carryover in the classroom.

Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:

Attention to adult-directed tasks was variable; he preferred self-directed play and needed frequent gentle redirection. Accepted visual schedule and simple choices.

Goals/targets this session:
1) Establish baseline for speech sound production
- During play-based interaction, overall intelligibility to the clinician was reduced in connected speech. Frequent omission of final consonants (e.g., "ki" for "kick," "do" for "dog"), and inconsistent marking of consonant blends ("top" for "stop," "pider" for "spider").
- Syllable shapes observed: strong use of CV and CVCV patterns; CVC and CCVC forms were less consistent.

2) Initial /f/ at isolation and syllable level
- Introduced /f/ as a "quiet lip bite air" sound. In isolation with visual model and mirror, client approximated /f/ in about half of attempts; often substituted /p/ or produced only airflow without lip contact.
- In CV syllables ("foo," "faa"), accuracy was low; productions frequently shifted to /p/ ("poo"). Maximal verbal and visual cues required.

3) Initial /s/ at isolation level
- Client demonstrated awareness of /s/ when modeled, occasionally labeling it as a "snake" sound. In isolation, /s/ often produced as /d/ or lateralized with extra airflow.
- With mirror and slowed modeling, he produced a recognizable /s/ in some attempts, but could not yet maintain consistency. Required maximal verbal cueing and some tactile cueing (finger to sides of mouth) to shape airflow.

4) Final consonant production (general)
- Probed final /p, m, t/ in CVC words ("map," "cat," "cup") in imitation. Client omitted final consonants in most attempts. When provided with segmented modeling and tapping for each sound, he could add a final consonant occasionally but not reliably.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build rapport and establish routine.
- Next session: continue shaping /f/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables; continue probing final consonants.
- Home practice: caregiver encouraged to model slow, clear productions of target words during daily routines (e.g., "feet," "soap," "cup") without pressure for the child to imitate; praise any clear attempts. No formal homework sheet provided today.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words)
- Targeted final /p/ and /t/ in simple CVC words (e.g., “cup, up, hat, hot”) in imitation. Client frequently omitted final consonants (“cu,” “ha”). Required frequent visual cues (pointing to mouth, tapping picture for the “end sound”) and slowed rate.

2. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level
- Practiced /f/ in isolation (“ffff”) and in simple syllables (“fuh, foo”). Client could approximate /f/ with moderate verbal cues to “bite your lip and blow,” but often substituted /p/ or /b/. At the word level (e.g., “fish, four, foot”), accuracy was low; productions were often “pish, bor.” Heavy modeling and mirror work used; client responded well to watching the clinician’s mouth.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Introduced /s/ in isolation using “snake sound” imagery. Client produced a prolonged /s/ sound with air mostly through the center of the tongue in some attempts, but often distorted to /th/ or stopped to /t/. At the syllable level (“see, soo”), accuracy was inconsistent even with maximal cues and models. Used gestural cue (finger in front of mouth like a snake) with some success.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed spontaneous productions in play (e.g., “mama, baby, go car”). Client produced a variety of CV and CVCV (reduplicated) shapes. In imitation tasks with picture cards, he could imitate most CV and CVCV forms, but often simplified CVC to CV.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to target final consonants in high-interest CVC words and build consistency at the imitated word level.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (p, t, m)
- Revisited final /p/, /t/, and added /m/ in CVC words (e.g., “cup, hat, ham, home”). In imitation with visual cueing (tapping the final sound on the picture), client produced final consonants with noticeably higher consistency than last week. Many words produced with a clear final sound, though sometimes released with an extra vowel (“cup-uh”). Emerging ability to self-correct when provided with a second model.

2. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level
- Practiced /f/ in isolation, CV syllables, and high-interest words (“fish, food, fun”). Client was able to sustain /f/ in isolation more consistently today, especially when looking in a mirror and following the cue “bite lip, blow air.” At the word level, approximations frequently still began with /p/ or /h/, but there were several accurate or near-accurate productions with moderate cues. Improvement noted in his willingness to attempt multiple repetitions.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and simple words
- Continued work on /s/ in isolation and in a small set of words (“sun, soup, sock”). Client produced a clearer /s/ in isolation more often today using the “snake sound” cue. At the word level, errors remained (e.g., “tun” for “sun,” “thock” for “sock”), but he was beginning to differentiate the target sound from /t/ when provided with auditory discrimination tasks. Needed maximal verbal and visual prompts to approximate /s/ in initial position.

4. Functional phrase attempts
- During play, encouraged simple two- and three-word phrases (e.g., “more cars,” “big truck”). Client imitated short phrases in many opportunities but often reduced or blurred final consonants. Intelligibility improved slightly when he slowed his rate with clinician modeling.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue at the imitated CVC word level for final consonants, aiming for more consistent, closed syllables.
- Maintain focus on shaping /f/ and /s/ in isolation and word-initial position with intensive modeling.
- Home practice: parent to play simple “sound games” focusing on final sounds (e.g., “I spy something that ends with /p/”) and to continue mirror practice for /f/ and /s/ a few minutes daily.

Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words (p, t, m, n)
- Expanded set of CVC targets (e.g., “cup, hat, ham, sun, moon, man”). In imitated word-level tasks, client produced final consonants with much greater consistency than previous sessions. Final /p/ and /t/ were usually present, though sometimes slightly aspirated or followed by a schwa. Final /m/ and /n/ were mostly accurate in imitation. Occasional spontaneous use of final consonants noted in play (e.g., “hat” while dressing a doll) without direct prompting.

2. Initial /f/ at word level
- Continued work on /f/ in words such as “fish, food, four, fun.” Client showed a noticeable increase in accurate or close approximations when imitating, especially when given a verbal cue (“lip bite”) plus a gesture. Substitution of /p/ occurred less frequently. Able to maintain /f/ at the beginning of the word in several consecutive trials for “fish” and “four.” Still required moderate cueing, but overall accuracy and confidence improved.

3. Initial /s/ at word level
- Targeted /s/ in a limited word set (“sun, soup, sock, seal”) using picture cards and a “snake sound” story. Client produced /s/ in isolation with good quality most of the time. At the imitated word level, he produced initial /s/ correctly or with mild distortion in many trials, though some productions remained /t/ or /th/ substitutions. Benefited from slower rate and models with exaggerated /s/ onset.

4. Early /s/ blends – awareness
- Briefly introduced /s/ blends (e.g., “snow, star, spoon”) at an awareness level only. Client listened and attempted to identify whether he heard “no” vs. “snow.” He could sometimes distinguish the presence of /s/ in minimal pairs but was not yet ready to consistently produce blends.

Plan / recommendations:
- For next sessions, continue strengthening final consonants; consider moving some well-established CVC words toward short carrier phrases (e.g., “my hat”).
- Continue to consolidate initial /f/ and /s/ in a small set of high-frequency words.
- Home practice: encourage use of clear final sounds in familiar words during routines (bath, mealtimes). Provide parent with picture cards for “fish, four, fun” and “sun, sock” to practice short, positive drill-play bursts.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Final consonants in CVC words – transition to carrier phrases
- Practiced familiar CVC words (e.g., “hat, cup, moon, sun”) first in imitation, then in short carrier phrases such as “my hat,” “in cup,” “big sun.” At the word level, final consonants were generally present and clear. In short phrases, he maintained final consonants in many attempts, though there were occasional omissions when the phrase was longer or faster. Benefited from pacing cues and pausing slightly before the final sound.

2. Initial /f/ – increased independence
- Continued with /f/ words (“fish, four, fun, food, feet”). Client produced /f/ accurately in most imitated words with only light verbal reminders. When cuing was faded, he was able to produce several correct /f/ words independently, especially in single-word naming tasks. Some reversion to /p/ occurred in spontaneous play, but he responded well to gentle reminders and self-corrected in a few instances.

3. Initial /s/ – word and simple phrase level
- Worked on /s/ initial words (“sun, sock, soup, seal”) and began short phrases (e.g., “sun up,” “sock on”). Client showed increased consistency with initial /s/ in imitation, with fewer /t/ substitutions. In short phrases, he often preserved the /s/ when the phrase was rehearsed and slowed down. Needed moderate visual cues when phrases were novel or when excited.

4. Early /s/ blends – imitated word level
- Introduced a small set of /s/ blends at the imitated word level (e.g., “star, spoon, slide”). Client was able to imitate some blends but tended to omit the /s/ (“tar, poon, lide”) or simplify the cluster. With backward chaining (e.g., “tar” → “star”) and hand cueing, he produced a few accurate blends, showing readiness to begin structured practice at this level.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen final consonants within short phrases and monitor consistency across activities.
- Expand the set of /f/ and /s/ words and continue to fade cues as tolerated.
- Begin regular, structured practice of a small number of /s/ blends using backward chaining.
- Home practice: parent to model short phrases with final consonants (e.g., “big cup,” “my hat”) and encourage slow, clear speech; send home a simple /s/ blend game with “star” and “spoon.”

Goals / session focus:
1. Establish /k/ in isolation
- Practiced "back sound" /k/ with use of tongue depressor for placement, mirror, and verbal cues ("tongue up and back").

- Attempted after isolation practice.
- Child often substituted /t/ ("tee" for "kee").

3. Initial /s/ in isolation
- Introduced "long, quiet air" /s/ using snake imagery and mirror.
- Child could briefly hold airflow but often added a voice or changed placement to /sh/ or /th/-like sounds.
- Required hand-over-hand cueing for airflow and lip retraction; no consistent independent /s/ yet.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed and elicited a variety of simple syllable shapes in play (e.g., "boo," "mama," "nana").
- Child produced CV and reduplicated CVCV forms easily; some difficulty with alternating CVCV (e.g., "bunny" became "buh-buh").

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to establish /k/ and /s/ at isolation and simple syllable levels before moving to words.
- Encourage parent to model slow, clear productions during daily routines without pressure for child to repeat.
- Provide parent with simple visuals (snake for /s/, "back sound" picture for /k/) and demonstrate how to praise any attempts at these sounds.
- Next session: continue placement work for /k/ and /s/ and begin probing for /k/ in a small set of high-interest words if ready.

Goals / Session Targets:

- Introduced /sh/ as “quiet sound.” Child tended to produce /s/ instead.
- With modeling and cue to round lips, child could approximate /sh/ in isolation intermittently, but not yet stable.

Plan/Recommendations:

Parent report:
Mom reported that she understands most of what he says, but others have a harder time. She notices he often leaves off the ends of words and says “tup” for “cup” and “tar” for “car.” No major changes since the evaluation.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned with some hesitation but separated from mom with reassurance. He was generally cooperative and curious, though he preferred to move quickly between toys. Needed intermittent redirection to stay with structured tasks but responded well to visual supports and turn-taking games.

Goals / session focus:
1. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, m, t/)
- Targeted final consonants in simple CVC words (e.g., “cup, map, cat, sit, mop”) at the imitation level.
- Client frequently omitted final consonants or substituted a neutral vowel (“cu-” for “cup”).
- With maximal verbal cues (e.g., “stop your sound,” “close your lips at the end”) and tactile cues on his chin/hand-over-hand tapping, he was able to mark some final sounds, but accuracy was inconsistent and required slow pacing and repetition.

2. Initial /k/ vs /t/ contrast (fronting)
- Practiced initial /k/ in isolation and in simple syllables (e.g., “ku, ki, ka”) using visual cues (tongue “back sound”) and mirror.
- Client often produced /t/ for /k/ in imitation, even with modeling.
- A few accurate /k/ productions emerged when paired with a strong cough-like cue and exaggerated model, but he was not yet consistent.

3. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Introduced “snake sound” /s/ in isolation with visual cue (finger for airflow) and mirror.
- Client was able to approximate a prolonged airflow in isolation with moderate verbal and gestural support, though he frequently added a vowel (“suh”).
- Attempted /s/ in CV syllables (e.g., “see, so, suh”), but productions often shifted to /d/ or /sh/; remained largely at isolation level today.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVCV)
- Observed spontaneous productions during play: mostly CV and CVCV reduplicated forms (e.g., “baba,” “mama,” “nana”), with fewer clear CVC words.
- Used simple picture routines to model CVCV (alternating) words (e.g., “puppy, baby”) and encouraged imitation; client could imitate the overall syllable pattern but individual sounds were often unclear.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue targeting final consonants in simple CVC words at the imitation level, using tapping and “stop your sound” cues.
- Continue establishing /k/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables before moving up to consistent word-level practice.
- Encourage parents to model slow, clear CVC words in daily routines (e.g., “cup, bed, hat”) without pressuring him to repeat; praise any attempts to mark the ending sound.

- Introduced additional 2-word phrases with /s/ words (e.g., “sun up,” “see soup”); he could imitate these with moderate cueing but rarely produced /s/ independently.

4. Short carrier phrases with multiple targets
- Used carrier phrases (e.g., “I see __,” “my __,” “big __”) to embed CVC and /k/ words.
- Client imitated several 2–3 word phrases with recognizable target sounds, particularly for /k/ and final /p, t/.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen consistency and independence at the word level for /s/ and final /k/, while increasing phrase-level practice for sounds that are more stable (/k/ initial, final /p, t/).
- Home: Encourage short, repeated phrases using familiar targets (e.g., “my car,” “big cup,” “see sun”), praising any self-corrections or clear endings.

Session 1

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Parent reported that child is “hard to understand” for unfamiliar listeners and often leaves off sounds at the ends of words. No prior speech therapy. Parent notices that /k/ and /g/ are usually replaced with /t/ and /d/ (e.g., “tat” for “cat”).
- No major changes in speech over the past few months per parent; family is motivated to start home practice.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- 4-year-old child transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from parent with reassurance.
- Once settled, child engaged well in play-based activities and followed simple directions. Attention was best with hands-on toys and picture books. Occasional silliness when tasks became more challenging, but generally redirectable.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: Establish /k/ in isolation and simple syllables
- Baseline probing showed consistent fronting of /k/ to /t/ in all positions.
- With direct modeling and visual cues (showing “back tongue” in mirror), child was able to approximate /k/ in isolation in a few trials; productions were often “t-k” or very light contact.
- In CV syllables (e.g., “koo, kah”), accuracy was low; child reverted to /t/ most of the time. Required maximal verbal and visual cueing.

Goal 2: Establish /g/ in isolation
- Similar pattern of fronting /g/ to /d/. In isolation, child produced a few close approximations of /g/ with simultaneous modeling.
- Needed repeated reminders to “lift tongue at the back” and to keep lips relaxed. Accuracy remained inconsistent.

Goal 3: Initial /k/ at imitated word level (emerging)
- Probed initial /k/ in simple words (e.g., “key, cow, car, cookie”). Child mostly produced /t/ (e.g., “tee” for “key”).
- With maximal modeling, segmentation (“k—ey”), and tactile cue under chin, child produced a few clearer /k/ sounds, but not yet consistent.

Goal 4: Final consonant production (p, m, t) in CVC words
- Screening during play showed frequent omission of final consonants (e.g., “bee” for “beep,” “boo” for “boom”).
- With model and visual cue (pointing to mouth for “end sound”), child could sometimes add a final /p/ or /m/, but needed frequent repetition and support.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue to focus on establishing /k/ and /g/ in isolation and simple syllables before moving more fully to word level.
- Introduce simple final consonant practice with high-interest words.
- Home practice: parent to model “back sounds” /k, g/ in a mirror for short, playful practice (1–2 minutes), emphasizing listening and watching rather than pushing for perfection.
- Next session: recheck /k/ and /g/ in isolation and CV syllables; continue to probe final consonants in CVC words.

Session 2

Parent/Caregiver Report:
- Parent reported child has been “showing off” new sounds at home but they still mostly hear /t/ for /k/ and /d/ for /g/ in words.
- Parent attempted mirror practice a few times; child was cooperative for short periods.

Behaviour/Engagement:
- Child transitioned smoothly today and appeared excited to come into the room.
- Good participation in structured play; attended well to picture cards and mirror work for short bursts. Needed occasional breaks with toys between drill tasks.

Therapy Goals & Observations:
Goal 1: /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Marked breakthrough compared to last week. With verbal cue (“tongue in the back”) and mirror, child produced a clear /k/ in isolation in most attempts.
- In CV combinations (e.g., “koo, kah, kee”), accuracy improved notably. Child was able to maintain the back tongue position with moderate verbal cueing and occasional visual reminder.

Goal 2: /g/ in isolation and CV syllables
- /g/ also more accurate today. Child produced several good /g/ sounds in isolation with model.
- In CV syllables (“goo, gah, gee”), productions were mixed; some reverted to /d/, but there were more successful trials than last session.

Goal 3: Initial /k/ at imitated word level
- Practiced simple words (key, cow, car, cookie, cat). Child more consistently initiated words with a recognizable /k/ when given a slow model and cue to “start with the back sound.”
- Still needed moderate support, but accuracy was noticeably better than baseline.

Goal 4: Final /p, m/ in CVC words (imitated)
- Targeted final consonants in high-interest words (pop, beep, boom, mom).
- With modeling and a visual cue (touching chin for end sound), child was able to add final /p/ and /m/ more often than last session, though omissions still occurred.

Plan/Recommendations:
- Continue strengthening /k/ and /g/ in CV syllables and initial word position, aiming for more automatic productions.
- Maintain short, fun final consonant practice.
- Home practice: parent to use a few target words (e.g., key, cow, pop, mom) and emphasize the “back sound” and the “end sound,” praising any attempts.
- Next session: consider expanding initial /k/ to a small set of words in simple carrier phrases if accuracy remains strong.

- Child was able to approximate /k/ in isolation with maximal verbal and visual cues in about half of attempts; often reverted to /t/ when excited.
- At CV level (e.g., “koo,” “kay”), productions were mostly /too/ and /tay/ despite simultaneous models.

3) Final consonant awareness (CVC words – early stops /p, t/)
- Introduced idea of “end sound” using picture cards and tapping for CVC words (e.g., “cup,” “cat”).
- Child could sometimes imitate final /p/ with direct modeling and slowed rate; final /t/ was usually omitted or replaced with a vowel.
- Required frequent verbal reminders to “add the end sound” and benefited from segmenting the word.

4) Initial /s/ at isolation level
- Introduced “long, quiet snake sound.”
- Child attempted /s/ in isolation with variable success; often produced /d/ or a short, distorted sound.
- Tolerated mirror work and watching clinician’s mouth; needed continuous cueing for airflow.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to target /k/ in isolation and CV syllables, final consonant marking in simple CVC words, and establish a clearer /s/ in isolation.
- Encourage parent to model slow, clear productions in play (e.g., emphasizing final sounds in words like “cup,” “hat”).
- Plan to gather additional baseline on /sh/ and blends in upcoming sessions.

Goals / Observations:
1) Initial /f/ at syllable and word level (imitation)
- Baseline probing completed today. At the isolated sound level, he could imitate a "quiet blowing" /f/ in about half of opportunities when given a mirror and verbal model.

- In isolation, he was able to sustain a short /s/ with combined verbal and gestural cues ("snake sound") in about half of attempts. Frequent stopping to /t/ or substitution with /sh/.

- Informal sampling during play indicated frequent omission of final consonants, particularly in CVC words (e.g., "ca" for "cat," "do" for "dog").

4) Baseline intelligibility / spontaneous speech
- During play-based conversation, intelligibility to the unfamiliar listener (SLP) was reduced; required frequent clarification and repetition.

Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:
- Child transitioned with some hesitation but separated from caregiver with brief reassurance. Once in the room, they engaged well in play-based activities and responded positively to praise and turn-taking games. Attention was best with hands-on toys and brief activities.

Goals / session focus:
1) Establish production of /k/ in isolation and simple syllables (back sound vs. front sound)
- Practiced /k/ in isolation using mirrors, tongue “back sound” description, and visual of a “cough” sound. Child could approximate /k/ in isolation intermittently with maximal verbal and visual cueing (often substituted /t/). 
- In CV syllables (e.g., “koo, kah”), productions were largely fronted to /t/ with occasional close approximations when cued to “lift the back of your tongue.” Accuracy low and inconsistent.

2) Initial /k/ at the imitated word level
- Trialed a small set of words (e.g., “key, car, cow”) in imitation. Child typically produced /t/ in place of /k/ (e.g., “tea” for “key”). With simultaneous production and direct placement cues, a few clearer /k/ productions were observed but not yet consistent.

3) Establish production of /s/ in isolation
- Introduced “snake sound” /s/ with mirror and visual of teeth together/air through. Child was interested in the snake theme but often produced /d/ or a short “th” sound instead. With maximal model and gestural cues, child produced a prolonged /s/ a few times but could not yet maintain the posture independently.

4) Functional word attempts in play
- Encouraged child to imitate simple, functional words during play (e.g., “go, up, more, ball”). Child imitated several single words, though many were reduced or unclear. Focus was on building willingness to imitate and shaping clearer consonants with high praise.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue targeting /k/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables with heavy modeling and visual supports. Maintain short, play-based trials.
- Caregiver coached to model “back sound” /k/ in a mirror at home using fun “cough” or “car” sounds, without pressuring for perfect accuracy.
- Next session: re-check /k/ and /s/ in isolation; consider introducing final /k/ in simple words if any consistent back sound emerges.

- Continued to encourage imitation of simple, functional words in play (e.g., “go, off, in, out, more, up”). Child imitated a variety of words with moderate success. Parent report:

Goals / session focus:

- In CV syllables (“koo, kah, kee”), accuracy was noticeably improved. Child was able to produce several accurate CV sequences in a row with pacing and repetition. Occasional fronting to /t/ still occurred but was less frequent.

- Trialed final /k/ in a few VC words (e.g., “back, book”) in imitation. Child often omitted or softened the final consonant, but there were a few clear final /k/ productions with maximal cues and segmenting (“ba…k”). This is a new skill and appears just emerging.

- Continued work on “snake sound.” Child produced /s/ with moderate cues (teeth together, slow air) more consistently than previous sessions. Still variable but improved ability to sustain the sound for 1–2 seconds.

Goals / session focus:

- Practiced a larger variety of initial /k/ words and phrases (e.g., “car, cookie, cup, coat, key,” “my car,” “big cookie,” “blue cup,” “coat on”). Child produced initial /k/ accurately in most imitated trials with minimal cues.
