Mom reported that she understands most of what her child says, but unfamiliar listeners often ask for repetitions. She noted that he "drops sounds" at the ends of words and sometimes says "t" for "k". No major changes since the initial evaluation.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client transitioned to the therapy room with some hesitation but separated from Mom with reassurance. He was curious about the toys and engaged well in play-based activities. Attention was variable but generally adequate for short, structured tasks with frequent breaks.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. Establish /k/ in isolation
- Targeted /k/ in isolation using tongue-back cues and visuals ("cough sound in the back"). Client was able to approximate /k/ with moderate to maximal verbal and visual cueing, with inconsistent accuracy. Often substituted /t/ for /k/. Continued need for simultaneous productions with SLP.

2. Initial /k/ in CV syllables (e.g., "koo," "kee")
- Practiced initial /k/ in simple CV syllables at the imitation level. Accuracy was low and productions frequently shifted to /t/. Client benefited from mirror work and hand cue to signal "back sound." Able to produce a few accurate exemplars with high support.

3. Final consonant production in CVC words (p, m, t)
- Introduced final consonant marking at the imitated word level with visuals (e.g., "map," "mat," "mom"). Client frequently omitted final sounds, though he occasionally marked them when provided with slowed speech and segmenting. Some success with final /m/ in high-interest words ("mom").

4. Functional phrase use
- Encouraged short, functional phrases in play (e.g., "my turn," "more cars"). Client mostly used 1–2 word utterances spontaneously and imitated simple 2-word combinations with models.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to focus on establishing /k/ in isolation and CV syllables with high levels of support. Maintain work on final consonant marking for a small set of familiar words. Caregiver encouraged to model clear final sounds in daily routines (e.g., emphasizing end sounds in "up," "in," "out").

Parent report:
Mom reported no major changes since last week. She has been trying to slow her speech and emphasize the ends of words as discussed. She noticed he sometimes attempts a "back sound" when prompted but often reverts to his usual pattern.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client entered the room more easily today and appeared comfortable with the routine. He participated well in turn-taking games and responded to visual schedules. Attention was adequate with brief movement breaks.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Continued work on /k/ in isolation using mirror, tongue-back cues, and tactile prompts to the throat. Accuracy improved compared to last session; he produced a clearer /k/ in isolation in a number of trials with moderate cueing. When moving to CV syllables ("koo," "kah"), accuracy was emerging but still inconsistent, with frequent /t/ substitutions.

2. Initial /k/ in high-interest words
- Introduced a small set of familiar /k/-initial words at the imitated word level (e.g., "car," "key," "cow"). Client was able to approximate /k/ in some trials when provided with a strong model and cue to "make the back sound," though consistency was limited. Needed moderate to maximal cues to maintain tongue retraction.

3. Final consonant production (p, m, t) in CVC words
- Revisited final consonants using picture cards and a "sound dot" visual for the end sound. Client showed slightly more awareness of the final sound and occasionally self-corrected when reminded to "add the last sound." Accuracy remained variable but there were more clear productions of final /m/ and /p/ in practiced words.

4. Two-word functional phrases
- Encouraged use of 2-word phrases (e.g., "more bubbles," "my car") during play. Client imitated many 2-word combinations with a model and spontaneously produced several short phrases when motivated.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue at the current levels: /k/ in isolation and CV syllables, and initial /k/ in a limited set of words at the imitated level. Maintain emphasis on final consonants in a few target CVC words. Caregiver encouraged to keep modeling "back sound" for /k/ in everyday words like "car" and "cookie" and to praise any attempts.

Parent report:
Mom shared that he is starting to say "car" a bit more clearly at home, especially when reminded. She still notices many "t" for "k" substitutions in other words. She also feels he is trying to copy how adults say words more often.

Behaviour/engagement:
Client was cheerful and eager to show the clinician a toy car he brought from home. He engaged well in structured table tasks for short periods and was easily re-engaged with visual supports when distracted. Participation was good with minimal protest.

Goals / therapy focus:
1. /k/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Client showed more consistent /k/ productions in isolation with moderate verbal and visual cueing. He required fewer repetitions to achieve a clear "back sound." In CV syllables, accuracy improved; he produced several correct "koo" and "kah" productions in imitation, though /t/ substitutions still occurred when he was excited or speaking quickly.

2. Initial /k/ in imitated words
- Practiced /k/ in a set of familiar words ("car," "key," "cow," "cup") at the imitated word level. Accuracy was higher than previous sessions when given a model plus reminder to "tap the back sound." He needed less cueing to initiate /k/ in these specific words and sometimes corrected himself after producing /t/.

3. Final consonants in CVC words (p, m, t)
- Continued work on final consonant marking using picture cards and segmenting ("ma—p"). Client demonstrated slightly increased consistency, especially for final /m/ and /p/. Final /t/ remained more challenging, with frequent omissions unless cued. He responded well to slow, choppy speech and finger tapping for each sound.

4. Short carrier phrases with targets
- Introduced simple carrier phrases such as "my ___" and "a ___" using /k/ words and CVC targets in play. Client imitated these short phrases with fair clarity; target sounds were more accurate in structured phrases than in spontaneous speech.

Plan / recommendations:
Given the emerging consistency with /k/ in isolation and CV syllables, will continue to strengthen /k/ at the word level in a small, familiar set and begin to trial more words if accuracy remains steady. Maintain practice with final consonants using a limited word list. Caregiver encouraged to use slow, segmented models and to praise any self-corrections.

Parent report:

Behaviour/engagement:

Goals / therapy focus:

- Client showed a meaningful increase in consistency for initial /k/ in his core word list ("car," "key," "cow," "cup," "coat"). With a model and a brief cue, he produced /k/ correctly in most trials. Occasional /t/ substitutions occurred, but he demonstrated more frequent self-correction when provided with a questioning look or "try again" cue.

2. Expansion of /k/ to additional words and simple phrases
- Introduced new /k/-initial words ("cake," "cat," "corn") at the imitated word level. Accuracy was variable but better than in earlier sessions, with clearer /k/ in several attempts. Trialed short phrases ("my car," "big cake") with direct models; client imitated these with moderate success, maintaining the /k/ in many productions.

3. Final consonants (p, m, t) in CVC words
- Continued work on final consonant marking. Client showed increased awareness of final sounds, occasionally pausing and adding the last sound after a cue. Final /m/ and /p/ were more consistent in a handful of practiced words (e.g., "map," "mom," "cup"). Final /t/ still required significant cueing and often reduced to a softer or omitted ending.

- Practiced 2–3 word functional phrases ("my turn," "more bubbles," "go car") during play, emphasizing clear initial and final sounds. Client imitated many of these with improved clarity compared to prior sessions, though spontaneous phrases remained less precise.

Plan / recommendations:

Maintain focus on final consonants, particularly /t/, using slow, segmented models. Caregiver encouraged to cue for "back sound" and to model 2–3 word phrases with clear beginning and ending sounds during play.

- Continued work on phrases such as "my car," "big cookie," "go car," "more cake." Client imitated these with fair accuracy, often maintaining the /k/ in the target word when given a model. When speaking more quickly, he occasionally reverted to /t/ or omitted final consonants.

- Revisited final consonants using a small set of picture cards and a "tap the last sound" strategy. - Encouraged 2–3 word requests and comments during play. Client produced many short phrases spontaneously, though speech clarity varied. With modeling, he was able to repeat phrases more clearly, particularly when focused on one or two target words.

Keep targeting final consonants, with specific attention to /t/, while recognizing that progress here is more gradual. Caregiver encouraged to continue modeling clear /k/ words and final sounds during daily routines and to pause to allow him time to self-correct.

Parent report:
Parent reported no major changes since the initial visit. She noted that familiar family members can usually understand the child in context, but new listeners still have difficulty, especially with words starting with /s/ and /f/. Parent is motivated to work on “clearer talking” at home.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned slowly but without distress. Needed a few minutes of free play to warm up, then participated with gentle redirection. Attention was variable; short structured activities (2–3 minutes) were best tolerated. Accepted verbal models and some visual cueing; hesitant with direct tactile cues to the face but allowed demonstration on a puppet.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /f/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Practiced /f/ sound in isolation using a “quiet lip” cue and mirror. Child could approximate /f/ in isolation in some attempts but frequently substituted /p/ or omitted airflow. In CV syllables (e.g., "fee, foo, faa"), productions were mostly /pV/ or /hV/. Required frequent verbal models, slowed rate, and visual cueing. A few clearer /f/ sounds emerged with maximal support.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation
- Targeted /s/ in isolation using a “snake sound” cue. Child produced /s/ with intermittent accuracy; many attempts were closer to /d/ or /t/. Needed repeated modeling, reminders to keep tongue behind teeth, and visual support using a mirror. Accuracy improved slightly when the clinician provided very slow, exaggerated productions for the child to copy.

3. CV and CVC syllable shapes
- Practiced simple CV (e.g., "me, no, boo") and CVC (e.g., "mom, pop") structures during play. CV forms were generally present and clear. CVC forms often had final consonant omission or vowel prolongation. When cued to “add the last sound,” child could sometimes add a consonant in imitation but did not yet do this independently.

4. Early word-level attempts with initial /f/ and /s/
- Briefly probed a small set of words ("sun, sock, fun, food") at the imitated word level. Child attempted all items but accuracy was low; initial consonants were often changed or omitted. These probes served as baseline; no expectation for independent accuracy at this time.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue to build consistency for /f/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables before pushing word-level practice.
- Encourage parent to model slow, clear “snake sound” /s/ and “quiet lip” /f/ during daily routines without pressuring the child to repeat.
- Next sessions will focus on stabilizing /f/ and /s/ in isolation and CV syllables with high support and short, play-based drills.

Plan / recommendations:

Parent report:
Caregiver shared that speech sounds remain hard to understand for unfamiliar listeners. Noted that child is beginning to try more words during play at home but often leaves off the ends of words (e.g., “ba” for “ball”). No major changes since the initial visit.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned slowly into the room but warmed up with preferred toys. Sat with the clinician at the table for short periods and then moved to the floor. Participated best in play‑based tasks with frequent breaks and visual supports.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /p/ and /b/ at the syllable and simple word level (imitation)
- Practiced CV syllables ("pa, po, bu, ba") and a small set of words ("ball, baby, pop, bye").
- Child imitated CV syllables with moderate verbal and visual cueing; accuracy roughly half the time, with frequent omission of the final sound or reduced lip closure.
- At the word level, child often produced only the vowel or approximations (e.g., “ah” for “pop”) even with modeling.

2. Final consonant production (CVC words: /p, m/)
- Targeted words such as “pop, up, mom, map” in play.
- Child primarily produced CV approximations (e.g., “ma” for “mom”), with occasional full CVC productions when given a slowed model and tapping cue on the table.
- Needed maximal verbal prompts (“close your lips at the end”) and visual models; accuracy was low and inconsistent.

3. Early /m/ and /n/ syllables and words
- Focused on nasal sounds in CV and CVCV patterns ("ma, me, moo, mama, nana").
- Child was more successful with /m/ in isolation and CV, imitating with moderate cues.
- CVCV words were often reduced (e.g., “ma” for “mama”).

4. Functional words in play
- Encouraged use of a few functional words ("more, up, go") during play activities.
- Child attempted these words when highly motivated (bubbles, cars) but needed the clinician to model each time and imitate immediately.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue targeting early bilabial sounds and final consonant production at the isolation, CV, and simple CVC word levels in imitation.
- Caregiver encouraged to model clear “pop, up, mom” during daily routines and praise any attempts, even if incomplete.
- Next sessions will focus on stabilizing initial /p, b, m/ in syllables and increasing awareness of “ending sounds” with tapping or clapping cues.

Child entered the room with interest and quickly joined the clinician in play. Participated in structured tasks when embedded in preferred games. Needed occasional prompts to stay with the activity but was generally cooperative and engaged.

Goals and session observations:
1. Initial /p, b, m/ in words and phrases
- Used familiar bilabial‑initial words and simple phrases.
- Initial bilabial sounds remained consistently accurate with minimal support.
- Skills at this level continue to be stable and well consolidated.

2. Final consonants /p, m/ in CVC words/phrases
- Practiced “pop, up, map, mom, ham” and related short phrases.
- Child showed a similar pattern to previous sessions: a mix of correct and omitted final consonants, with better accuracy when given clear verbal and visual cues.
- No notable change in cueing needs or independence compared to the last note.

3. Early alveolar /t, d/ in CV/CVC
- Continued work on “ta, tee, two, top, dad” with mirror feedback.
- Child produced some approximations but remained inconsistent and dependent on modeling.
- Skill level is consistent with prior sessions.

4. Two‑word combinations
- Encouraged functional phrases (e.g., “more bubbles,” “my ball,” “go car”) throughout play.
- Child imitated several phrases with support; spontaneous phrase use remained limited and often reduced.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue with the current therapy plan, allowing more time for consolidation of final consonant production and early alveolar sounds.
- Maintain emphasis on functional communication by embedding short phrase practice into natural play routines.
- Caregiver encouraged to keep modeling clear, slow speech with emphasized endings and to expand on the child’s single‑word utterances into short phrases during everyday activities.

Parent report:
Caregiver shared that they are understanding most of the child's words at home but that unfamiliar listeners still have difficulty, especially with words that start with /s/ and /f/. No major changes noted since the initial visit. Caregiver is motivated to work on clearer speech for preschool and daily routines.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned into the room with some initial hesitation but warmed up with familiar toys. Attended well to structured activities for short periods (3–4 minutes) with frequent breaks. Enjoyed turn-taking games and was generally cooperative with imitation tasks.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /f/ at the syllable and word level
- Practiced /f/ in isolation using mirror and "quiet air" cues. Child was able to imitate /f/ in isolation in about half of opportunities, often substituting /p/ or omitting the sound.
- At the CV syllable level ("fa, foo, fee"), accuracy was low and required maximum verbal and visual cueing (model, slowed rate, emphasis on lip bite). A few clear productions were achieved with hand-over-hand placement cues.
- Introduced a small set of initial /f/ words ("fish, food, fun"). Child could approximate these in imitation with significant support; productions were often "pish," "pood."

2. Initial /s/ at the syllable level
- Targeted /s/ in isolation with a snake sound cue. Child could sustain a short /s/ with direct modeling and reminders to keep the tongue behind the teeth, but this was inconsistent.
- Moved into simple CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"). Accuracy was variable; frequent substitutions with /d/ or distorted /s/ were observed. Required maximal verbal and visual cues, including mirror work.

3. Early /s/ blends at the imitation level
- Briefly probed /s/ blends in imitation ("spa, stah") to gauge baseline. Child typically omitted the /s/ (e.g., "pa" for "spa").
- Used auditory bombardment and minimal pair listening ("pa" vs "spa") to build awareness. Child was able to point to pictures when hearing the difference in some trials but did not yet consistently adjust their own productions.

4. Final consonant production (p, m)
- In play, observed occasional omission of final consonants (e.g., "bee" for "beep"). With immediate modeling and cues, child could add a final /p/ or /m/ in some imitation trials.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to establish accurate /f/ and /s/ in isolation and simple syllables before expanding to more words.
- Encourage caregiver to model slow, clear productions of everyday words with /f/ and /s/ (e.g., "fish," "soap") without pressuring the child to repeat.
- Next sessions will focus on building consistency for initial /f/ and /s/ at the syllable level and re-checking final consonant use in simple CVC words.

Parent report:
Caregiver reported that they have been practicing the "snake sound" and "quiet air" at home a few times per week. They notice the child sometimes tries to copy the sounds during play but still sounds "a bit mumbly" in longer words. No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child separated from caregiver more easily today and joined the therapy space with minimal prompting. Demonstrated good engagement with turn-taking games and tolerated brief structured drill when embedded in play. Occasional distraction by toys but easily redirected with visual supports.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level
- Reviewed /f/ in isolation; child produced a clearer /f/ in most attempts when using the mirror and a cue to bite the lip and blow. Fewer substitutions to /p/ compared to last session.
- At the CV syllable level ("fee, foo, fa"), accuracy improved with moderate verbal cues; child was able to produce several accurate syllables in a row when focused.
- Introduced 4 target words ("fish, fun, five, food") at the imitated word level. Child produced recognizable /f/ in the initial position for some trials, though still inconsistent. Required reminders to slow down and keep the lip-bite.

2. Initial /s/ in isolation and CV syllables
- Practiced sustained /s/ in front of a mirror with the "snake" cue. Child was able to produce a longer, more consistent /s/ sound today with fewer distortions.
- In simple syllables ("see, so, suh"), child showed more consistent tongue placement when provided with moderate verbal and visual cues. Still occasional substitutions with /d/, but less frequent.

3. Early /s/ blends (listening and imitation)
- Continued auditory discrimination tasks contrasting words with and without /s/ (e.g., "pin" vs "spin"). Child correctly identified the clinician’s target more often than last session, indicating increased awareness.
- In imitation, child still tended to drop the /s/ ("pin" for "spin"), but showed a few emerging attempts to add a quiet /s/ before the consonant when given slowed models and segmented practice ("s" + "pin").

4. Final consonant production (p, m)
- Practiced a small set of CVC words in play ("map, mop, beep, pop"). Child produced final /p/ and /m/ in some imitated trials when cued to "keep the sound at the end." Still inconsistent without cues.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to strengthen initial /f/ and /s/ accuracy at the syllable and simple word level with moderate cueing.
- Encourage caregiver to use mirror practice for /f/ and /s/ at home for brief, play-based trials.
- Next session, consider expanding the /f/ word list and re-visit /s/ blends with segmented practice if initial /s/ remains more stable.

Parent report:
Caregiver shared that the child has started saying "fish" and "five" more clearly during daily routines and will occasionally correct themselves after hearing the caregiver’s model. They still notice difficulty with /s/ in words like "sun" and "soap." No concerns about hearing or health.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child entered the room willingly and quickly engaged with preferred toys. Maintained attention for structured speech tasks for slightly longer periods than previous sessions, especially when tasks were embedded in games. Responded well to visual cues and praise.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /f/ at word level
- Reviewed /f/ in isolation briefly; child produced accurate /f/ with minimal prompts.
- Focused on initial /f/ words ("fish, four, five, fun, food, fox") at the imitated word level. Child produced clear /f/ in most attempts with light reminders to keep lips in place.
- Began to trial initial /f/ in short carrier phrases ("my fish," "more food") with clinician models. Child was able to imitate some phrases with recognizable /f/ but needed occasional cueing to maintain the sound in connected speech.

2. Initial /s/ at word level
- Practiced /s/ in isolation and then moved into a small set of initial /s/ words ("sun, soup, sock, soap, seat") at the imitated word level.
- Child demonstrated better tongue placement compared to earlier sessions. Some productions were accurate, though there were still substitutions with /d/ or distorted /s/ on more challenging words.
- Visual cues (mirror, pointing to teeth) and slowed models helped improve accuracy; child could sometimes self-correct when given a second chance.

3. Early /s/ blends (segmented practice)
- Continued awareness-building with minimal pair listening (e.g., "top" vs "stop"). Child correctly identified the clinician’s word in many trials, suggesting improved perception.
- Practiced segmented production ("s" + "top" → "stop") in imitation. Child was able to produce a faint /s/ before the consonant in a few attempts when heavily supported, but consistency remained low.

4. Final consonant production (p, m, t)
- Practiced CVC words ("map, mop, mat, boat, feet") in imitation. Child produced final consonants more reliably when cued to "tap" the last sound.
- Some spontaneous words in play included clearer final consonants, though omissions were still present when speech was faster.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to build consistency for initial /f/ at the word level and carryover into short phrases.
- Maintain focus on initial /s/ words, gradually increasing the set as accuracy improves.
- Keep /s/ blends in segmented imitation only at this time, with emphasis on awareness and slow productions.
- Home practice: caregiver to practice a short list of /f/ and /s/ words in a playful context, using clear models and gentle reminders rather than repeated drilling.

Parent report:
Caregiver reported that the child is using "fish," "food," and "sun" more clearly at home, and family members are commenting that they can understand those words better. They still find /s/ blends very hard (e.g., "stop" still sounds like "top"). No new concerns.

Behaviour/engagement:
Child transitioned smoothly and appeared eager to participate. Sat at the table for short structured tasks and then moved into play-based practice. Needed occasional breaks but returned to tasks with encouragement. Enjoyed choosing picture cards and stickers as rewards.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /f/ in words and short phrases
- Child produced initial /f/ in familiar words ("fish, four, five, food, fun") with good consistency in imitation and some emerging spontaneous use during play (e.g., naming "fish" without a model).
- Practiced short carrier phrases ("my fish," "more food," "big fox") in imitation. Child was generally able to maintain the /f/ sound, with only occasional reminders needed.
- Reduced cueing overall compared to prior sessions; mainly required a brief visual or verbal reminder when errors occurred.

2. Initial /s/ in words and emerging phrases
- Targeted initial /s/ words ("sun, soup, sock, soap, seat, sand") at the imitated word level. Child showed clearer /s/ productions today, with fewer substitutions.
- Introduced simple two-word combinations ("sun up," "sock on") in imitation for some targets. Child was able to maintain /s/ in a portion of these attempts with moderate cueing.

3. /s/ blends at the imitated word level
- Practiced a limited set of /s/ blends ("stop, spot, spin, snow") in imitation using segmented modeling ("s" + "top" → "stop").
- Child produced /s/ blends with partial success. Frequently, the /s/ was still omitted, but there were more attempts to include a quiet /s/ at the beginning.
- Visual supports (e.g., snake picture) and slowed rate helped; however, consistency remains low, and high levels of cueing are still needed.

4. Final consonant production (p, m, t) in words
- Practiced CVC words ("map, mop, mat, feet, boat, pop") in imitation and brief play tasks.
- Child produced final consonants more reliably when focused and given a cue ("don’t let it fall off"). Some carryover into spontaneous play was noted but still inconsistent.

Plan/recommendations:
- Maintain focus on consolidating accurate /f/ and /s/ productions at the word and short phrase level.
- Continue practicing a small set of /s/ blends in segmented imitation, emphasizing awareness rather than speed.
- Home program: caregiver to use picture cards or objects for 5–10 minutes of playful practice, highlighting clear /f/ and /s/ at the beginning of words and praising any attempts at blends.

- Practiced simple CV (e.g., "moo," "bee") and early CVC (e.g., "map," "mom") forms during play. Child produced CV syllables more consistently than CVC. Final consonants were often omitted or replaced with a neutral vowel (e.g., "ma" for "map"). With maximal modeling and slowed rate, the child was able to mark some final /m/ in imitation.

3) Initial /m/ and /b/ at single-word level (imitation)
- Introduced a small set of high-interest words ("more," "me," "ball," "baby"). Child imitated approximations for "more" and "me" in several trials with hand-over-hand sign support and verbal cues. Initial /m/ was more reliable than /b/. Accuracy remained low overall, but the child showed willingness to attempt targets.

4) Functional requesting with vocalizations
- Encouraged the child to pair a sound or word approximation with reaching or signing for desired items (bubbles, cars). Child often relied on gestures alone but, with prompting, occasionally added a vocalization (e.g., "muh" for "more"). Heavy cueing was required for consistent vocal attempts.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to build comfort with imitation and expand early consonant repertoire in isolation and CV syllables, focusing on /m/ and /b/. Caregiver encouraged to model simple CV words during daily routines (e.g., "ma," "ba," "more") without pressure to repeat. Next sessions will continue at isolation and CV/CVC syllable levels with play-based tasks to increase imitation and participation.

Goals/observations:
1. Final consonant production (CV → CVC) in play
- Targeted final /p/, /m/, and /t/ at the imitated word level (e.g., up, cup, map, mom, mat) using picture cards and toys.
- Child frequently omitted final consonants in spontaneous attempts (e.g., "cu" for cup, "ma" for map).
- With direct imitation and visual cueing (tapping the table for the last sound), child produced final consonants accurately in approximately 30–40% of opportunities.

2. Initial /p/ and /b/ in single words
- Practiced initial /p/ and /b/ in CV and CVC words (pea, bee, ball, puppy) mostly at the imitated word level.
- Child was more consistent with /b/ than /p/, with /p/ often produced with a weak burst or approximated as /b/.
- With a mirror and verbal cues to “pop your lips,” accuracy was around 50–60% in imitation; spontaneous productions were limited.

3. Syllable shapes (CV and CVCV)
- Observed spontaneous CV (e.g., "ma," "ba," "no") and reduplicated CVCV forms (e.g., "mama," "dada") during play.
- Imitated a small set of alternating CVCV words (e.g., "bunny," "baby") with mixed clarity; at least one consonant and vowel per syllable was usually present, but some consonants were distorted or substituted.

4. Early /k/ production in isolation
- Introduced back sound /k/ in isolation using mouth model and verbal cueing ("tongue back" sound).
- Child was able to approximate /k/ in isolation in about 3/10 trials with maximal visual and tactile cueing (touching under chin, pointing to throat).
- No consistent carryover into words yet.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to target final consonants at the imitated word level using high-interest toys and movement to maintain engagement.
- Maintain focus on strengthening initial /p/ and /b/ with mirror work and exaggerated lip popping.
- Encourage parent to model and emphasize final sounds at home in simple words (e.g., "up," "pop," "map"), praising any close approximations rather than insisting on perfection.
- Continue introductory work on /k/ in isolation, keeping expectations low and focusing on awareness of tongue-back placement.

- Child continued to demonstrate relatively strong /f/ productions at the imitated word level ("fish, four, feet, fun, food"). Occasional errors still occurred, particularly when the child was moving quickly or distracted.
- In short, imitated phrases ("my fish," "more food"), child often maintained the /f/ sound but sometimes reduced the phrase to a single word.

2. Initial /s/ at word and early phrase level (imitation)
- /s/ word-level practice ("sun, sock, soap, soup") remained similar to previous session. Child produced a number of accurate /s/ words with moderate cues but continued to have some inconsistent substitutions.
- Phrase-level attempts ("big sun," "my soap") were practiced in imitation. Child could repeat a few short phrases but often dropped words or reduced the phrase, suggesting this level is still emerging.

3. /s/ blends at the imitated word level
- Continued work on /s/ blends ("spoon, spider, star, stop"). Productions generally resembled single-consonant onsets (e.g., "poon" for "spoon").
- With segmented models and tactile cues, the child occasionally produced a brief /s/-like sound before the word, but this remained inconsistent.

4. Final consonants in CVC words (imitation)
- Practiced final /p, m, t/ in a small set of CVC words ("pop, map, mat, seat"). Child was able to include the final sound in several imitated attempts when cued to “close lips” or “tap tongue.”
- No clear increase in spontaneous final consonants was noted compared to last session.

Plan/recommendations:
- Continue to reinforce accurate /f/ and /s/ at the word level and gently expand phrase-level work as tolerated.
- Maintain current level of difficulty for /s/ blends and final consonants, focusing on consistency rather than increasing complexity.
- Parent encouraged to continue modeling and expanding the child’s phrases at home (e.g., adding one word to the child’s utterance) while highlighting clear target sounds.

3. /s/ blends at the imitated word level and emerging phrase level

- Continue to support /f/ and /s/ in both words and carrier phrases, with a focus on increasing spontaneous use.

Parent/caregiver report:

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /f/ at syllable and word level
- Practiced /f/ in isolation and simple CV syllables ("fa, foo, fee"). Client produced /f/ in isolation with moderate verbal and visual cues (lip-biting cue, mirror). At the imitated word level (e.g., "fan, food, feet, fish"), productions were emerging but inconsistent. Frequent substitutions to /p/ or omission of the fricative were noted. Client benefited from slowed rate and “blowing air” cue.

2. Initial /s/ at syllable and word level
- Targeted /s/ in isolation and in simple CV syllables ("see, soo, sah"). Client was able to approximate /s/ in isolation in some trials with strong visual modeling and verbal cues to “keep tongue behind teeth.” At the imitated word level ("sun, soup, sock, soap"), accuracy was low; errors included /d/ substitutions and occasional lateral airflow. Client responded best when watching clinician’s mouth closely.

3. Early /s/ blends – awareness and imitation
- Introduced /s/ blends at the imitated word level using a listening and minimal-pair approach (e.g., "pin/spin, pot/spot"). Focus was on awareness of the “extra sound” rather than consistent production. Client was able to point to pictures to show whether the clinician said the short or long word with support, but productions of blends were mostly reduced (e.g., "top" for "stop"). Required maximal verbal and visual cues; no independent blend productions.

4. Syllable shapes (CV, CVC, CVCV) in play
- During play, client produced a variety of CV and some CVCV (same consonant) shapes (e.g., "mama, dada, baba"). Final consonants in CVC words were often omitted or reduced in spontaneous attempts. Used modeling and immediate imitation to encourage full CVC forms (e.g., "cup, map"), with limited consistency.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue targeting /f/ and /s/ at the imitated syllable and word level with strong visual models and slowed rate.
- Maintain early exposure to /s/ blends through listening and simple imitation but remain primarily at single-consonant word level.
- Encourage caregiver to model clear CVC words in routines (e.g., bath, bed, cup) and pause for client to imitate. Provide caregiver with a short list of practice words for /f/ and /s/ to try in short, playful bursts rather than drills.

Goals / session focus:
1. Initial /f/ – imitated word level
- Reviewed /f/ in isolation, then moved to imitated words ("fish, fan, feet, four, food"). Compared to previous session, client produced clearer /f/ in more trials, particularly in high-interest words like "fish" and "food." Still showed occasional stopping to /p/, but with verbal cue “bite lip and blow,” client could usually self-correct on a second attempt. Accuracy and consistency were noticeably improved from baseline.

2. Initial /s/ – imitated word level
- Practiced /s/ words ("sun, soup, sock, seat, soap") in imitation. Client produced more stable /s/ airflow than last session with direct visual modeling and a “snake sound” cue. Still needed moderate cueing, but there were more correct productions per set, and fewer substitutions to /d/. Client attempted to watch the clinician’s mouth independently more often.

3. Early /s/ blends – imitated word level
- Continued minimal pair activities (e.g., "top/stop, pin/spin, pot/spot"). Client continued to reduce blends in spontaneous attempts, but in structured imitation with segmented cuing ("s" + "top"), could sometimes approximate the full blend. This is a small improvement from prior session where blends were almost always reduced. Accuracy remains emerging but movement toward fuller cluster production is evident.

4. Final consonant production – CVC words in imitation
- Practiced simple CVC words with visual cueing for final consonants (e.g., "cup, map, cat, bus"). Client was more willing to attempt full CVC shapes and occasionally maintained the final sound without needing repetition. Still requires frequent reminders and modeling, but there were several clearly produced CVC words across play.

Plan / recommendations:
- Continue focusing on /f/ and /s/ at the imitated word level, consolidating improved accuracy.
- Maintain structured practice with segmented /s/ blends ("s" + word) a few times per session.
- Provide caregiver with picture cards for 5–6 target /f/ and /s/ words and model how to cue “bite lip and blow” and “snake sound.”
- Continue short CVC practice at home with simple words, emphasizing final sounds with gentle repetition, not correction.

- Introduced simple /s/ + stop sequences (e.g., /s/ + /t/, /s/ + /p/) using toy cars and “start/stop” play. Child had difficulty maintaining both sounds, typically omitting the /s/ or reducing to a single consonant. Work remained at highly supported imitation; no reliable word-level productions observed.

4. Early word-level practice with functional words
- Briefly attempted a few high-interest words (e.g., “fish,” “sun”) to probe word-level accuracy. Child could imitate overall word shapes but often neutralized target sounds, resulting in reduced clarity. No data taken for accuracy; this was primarily exploratory.

Plan / recommendations:
Continue to focus on establishing more consistent /f/ and /s/ productions in isolation and simple syllables with high levels of support. Encourage caregiver to practice “lip bite and blow” for /f/ and “long, quiet air” for /s/ during daily routines (e.g., bath time, snack). Next sessions will continue at the sound and syllable level before moving more fully into single words.
